pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this secondary analysis of 29 spontaneously breathing ARDS patients on pressure support ventilation, higher PEEP individualized by EIT reduced tidal recruitment/derecruitment (R/D) compared to standard PEEP, with R/D associated with lower PEEP, more negative transpulmonary pressures, higher respiratory drive/effort, and non-focal infiltrates; these findings suggest that EIT-guided PEEP titration may help minimize injurious tidal R

pubmed.title: Severe Preoperative Infection and Postoperative Acute Kidney Injury in Children: A Two-Hit Risk Factor for Mortality.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study, children with severe preoperative infection had over twice the risk of postoperative acute kidney injury (AKI), and the combination of both conditions increased 30-day mortality risk ninefold, highlighting the critical need for heightened perioperative vigilance and prevention strategies in this high-risk pediatric population.

pubmed.title: Machine-learning algorithms identifies sTREM1 has a key biomarker for outcome prediction in critically ill.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, externally validated study found that plasma sTREM-1 is a strong, standalone biomarker for predicting mortality and major adverse kidney events in critically ill patients, outperforming traditional severity scores and matching machine learning models, suggesting its potential clinical utility for risk stratification in the ICU.

pubmed.title: Red Cell Transfusion in Critically Ill and Noncritically Ill Patients With Acute Myocardial Infarction and Anemia-A Subanalysis of the Myocardial Ischemia and Transfusion (MINT) Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large MINT trial subanalysis, a restrictive transfusion strategy in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia—whether critically ill or not—did not significantly increase 30-day mortality or death/MI compared to a liberal strategy, suggesting restrictive transfusion thresholds are generally safe in this population. This supports the use of restrictive transfusion practices in AMI patients with anemia, including those in the ICU.

pubmed.title: The Prevalence of Fibromyalgia in the General Population and At-Risk Subpopulations: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and meta-analysis found that fibromyalgia affects approximately 1.4% of the general population worldwide (very low certainty), but prevalence is much higher in individuals with autoimmune, inflammatory, and chronic pain conditions, highlighting the need for targeted screening and resource allocation in these at-risk groups.

pubmed.title: Beyond recovery: long-term cardiovascular risks after severe COVID-19 requiring intensive care.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, nationwide matched cohort study shows that survivors of severe COVID-19 requiring ICU care have significantly increased risks of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and all-cause mortality over three years, highlighting the need for long-term cardiovascular monitoring in this population.

pubmed.title: Interaction Between Admission Hemoglobin and Sex on ICU Mortality in Patients With Moderate-to-Severe Traumatic Brain Injury: A Retrospective Bicentric Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study of moderate-to-severe TBI patients, higher admission hemoglobin was associated with lower ICU mortality, but there was no significant interaction between sex and hemoglobin on mortality risk, suggesting that sex-specific transfusion thresholds based solely on admission hemoglobin are not warranted; further prospective research is needed.

pubmed.title: Prophylactic interventions to prevent rebound pain after peripheral nerve block in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and network meta-analysis of 24 RCTs (2130 patients) found that intravenous dexamethasone is the most effective prophylactic intervention for preventing rebound pain after peripheral nerve block, with moderate certainty evidence supporting its use as a first-line strategy; perineural dexamethasone and dexmedetomidine also delay onset and prolong analgesia, but further high-quality studies are needed for alternative approaches.

pubmed.title: Potentially Surgical Digestive Complications in Patients With Status Epilepticus: Insights From the ICTAL Registry.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter ICU registry study, potentially surgical digestive complications occurred in 1.6% of status epilepticus patients—most often in those with refractory SE and its treatments—and were independently associated with a more than threefold increase in hospital mortality, highlighting the need for vigilance and early recognition in this high-risk group.

pubmed.title: Does transthoracic impedance influence termination and recurrence of ventricular fibrillation during impedance-compensated shocks for Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest? A retrospective study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective study found that, even with impedance-compensated defibrillation, transthoracic impedance ≥100Ω is associated with decreased likelihood of terminating ventricular fibrillation/pulseless VT in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, though it does not affect recurrence risk; this suggests high TTI remains a barrier to effective shock delivery and may warrant clinical attention.

pubmed.title: US Air Quality Index and respiratory health outcomes: background, knowledge gaps, and research prioritization.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This research statement highlights significant evidence gaps regarding the effectiveness of the US Air Quality Index (AQI) in improving respiratory health outcomes, especially among individuals with respiratory disease, and prioritizes research to optimize AQI structure, communication, and clinical implementation; addressing these gaps is crucial for ensuring AQI messaging leads to meaningful health benefits.

pubmed.title: MRI-based quantitative profiling of hypoxic-ischemic brain injury in postanoxic status epilepticus and other EEG patterns.
pubmed.bottom_line: This retrospective two-center study found that comatose post-cardiac arrest patients with postanoxic status epilepticus (SE) had less severe hypoxic-ischemic brain injury on quantitative MRI than those with generalized periodic discharges or malignant EEG patterns, suggesting that SE may not indicate as poor a prognosis as previously thought; these findings may influence prognostication and management decisions in this population.

pubmed.title: Symptoms of anxiety and depression after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: an exploratory dyadic analysis of survivors and their cohabiting spouses or partners from the BOX trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This exploratory substudy from the BOX trial found that symptoms of anxiety and depression at three months post–out-of-hospital cardiac arrest were similar between survivors and their cohabiting partners, with higher age linked to fewer symptoms in survivors; these findings highlight the need for psychological support for both survivors and their partners, regardless of who experienced the cardiac arrest.

pubmed.title: Time course of energy expenditure in persistent critical illness: a prospective multicentre study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, multicenter prospective study found that energy expenditure in patients with persistent critical illness (ICU stay ≥10 days) follows a biphasic pattern, peaking around day 10 before declining, highlighting the need to adjust nutritional strategies over time; the evidence is robust and clinically relevant for optimizing metabolic support in prolonged ICU care.

pubmed.title: Development of a deep-learning model for classification of intensivists' extubation decisions using ventilator graphic monitor images: a prospective observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study developed a deep-learning model using ventilator graphic monitor images to classify intensivists' extubation decisions, with the best model (MobileNetV3-Large) achieving high accuracy (AUC 0.900) in a single-center cohort; while promising, further validation is needed before clinical implementation, but such tools could support less experienced providers in making extubation decisions.

pubmed.title: Fast Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing for Gram-Negative Bacteremia: The FAST Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized trial, rapid phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing for gram-negative bacteremia did not significantly improve 30-day clinical outcomes compared to standard testing, suggesting that while faster results are achievable, their clinical benefit in high-resistance settings may be limited when robust stewardship is present.

pubmed.title: Decompression with or without Duraplasty for Chiari I and Syringomyelia.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter, cluster-randomized controlled trial in children with Chiari I malformation and syringomyelia, decompression with duraplasty did not significantly increase surgical complications compared to decompression alone, and while duraplasty led to greater syrinx reduction and fewer repeat surgeries, overall clinical improvement and quality of life were similar; larger studies are needed to clarify the clinical benefits and risks.

pubmed.title: Zanidatamab with and without Tislelizumab in HER2-Positive Gastroesophageal Cancer.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3 trial, zanidatamab (with or without tislelizumab) plus chemotherapy significantly improved progression-free survival compared to trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in HER2-positive advanced gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma, and the addition of tislelizumab also significantly prolonged overall survival; these findings support zanidatamab-based regimens as a new standard first-line option.

foamed.title: ECG Pointers: MVC with Spinal Injury
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This emDocs article is an educational case-based discussion focused on ECG interpretation in the context of a motor vehicle collision with suspected spinal cord injury, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Individualized blood pressure and fluid therapy in esophagectomy
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a randomized controlled trial of 100 esophagectomy patients, extending individualized goal-directed fluid therapy and blood pressure management beyond surgery did not reduce postoperative complications or length of stay compared to standard care, despite increased interventions such as higher fluid and norepinephrine administration and slightly higher mean arterial pressure.

foamed.title: HI-PEITHO: More negative data on invasive therapy for higher risk PEs
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the HI-PEITHO trial, ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis combined with anticoagulation did not demonstrate a significant improvement in patient outcomes compared to anticoagulation alone in adults with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism, adding to the growing body of negative data regarding invasive therapies for this patient population.

foamed.title: Extravasation injuries
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article reviews extravasation injuries in children, describing their causes, risk factors, clinical presentation, and potential complications, but does not present new clinical research data or original study results.

foamed.title: History of epidural needles and catheters
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides a historical and technical overview of the development, design considerations, and clinical use of epidural needles and catheters, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Survival effect of prehospital emergency anaesthesia in major trauma
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides commentary on the ongoing controversy and complexity surrounding prehospital emergency anaesthesia (PHEA) in major trauma, noting that while airway control is fundamental, current evidence is conflicting and no clear survival benefit has been demonstrated, with only one major randomized trial showing improved neurological but not mortality outcomes.

foamed.title: CT Case 107
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational case describes a 60-year-old male with a CT-confirmed hangman fracture—bilateral acute undisplaced fractures of the pars interarticularis of C2—emphasizing that such injuries commonly result from high-velocity hyperextension, constitute 4–7% of cervical spine fractures, and are associated with neurological impairment in approximately 25% of cases.

foamed.title: A Randomized Trial of Shared Decision-Making in Code Status Discussions.
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a multi-centre cluster-randomized trial, structured shared decision-making interventions—including didactic teaching, observation with feedback, and a decision aid—led to higher rates of DNR selection and reduced patient uncertainty during code status discussions compared to usual care, though applicability to emergency department settings remains uncertain due to study limitations.

foamed.title: MDCalc Wars: sPESI vs Bova Score — PE Risk Stratification Made Practical
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational review explains that the sPESI score is most useful for identifying low-risk pulmonary embolism patients who may be suitable for discharge, while the Bova score helps detect normotensive patients at higher risk for early complications, emphasizing that clinical judgment should always guide final management decisions.

foamed.title: Tenecteplase versus standard medical treatment for basilar artery occlusion within 24 h (TRACE-5): a multicentre, prospective, randomised, open-label, blinded-endpoint, superiority, phase 3 trial.
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the TRACE-5 trial, intravenous tenecteplase administered within 24 hours for confirmed basilar artery occlusion improved excellent functional outcomes at 90 days compared to standard care (38% vs. 29%, NNT ≈ 11) without increasing symptomatic hemorrhage or mortality, though generalizability is limited by single-ethnicity and biosimilar formulation.

foamed.title: Acute Liver Failure
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational blog post reviews the definition, epidemiology, and clinical recognition of acute liver failure, emphasizing its rarity, high morbidity and mortality, and the importance of early diagnosis and referral, but does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: Fast Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing for Gram-Negative Bacteremia: The FAST Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized trial, rapid phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing for gram-negative bacteremia did not significantly improve 30-day clinical outcomes compared to standard testing, suggesting that while faster results are achievable, their clinical benefit in high-resistance settings may be limited when robust stewardship is present.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) for gram-negative bacteremia did not improve clinical outcomes compared to standard AST, though it did speed up antibiotic escalation or deescalation decisions.

- Study type: Open-label randomized clinical trial
- Population/setting: Hospitalized adults and children with gram-negative bacilli bloodstream infections at 7 medical centers in Greece, India, Israel, and Spain.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Rapid, phenotypic AST directly from positive blood cultures plus standard susceptibility testing versus standard susceptibility testing alone.
- Primary endpoints: Desirability of outcome ranking (DOOR) at day 30 (alive without deleterious events, alive with deleterious events, and death).
- Key results: The probability that DOOR outcomes were more favorable in the rapid testing group was 48.8% (95% CI, 45.3%-52.4%), indicating no superiority; median time to antibiotic escalation or deescalation was faster by 14 hours (95% CI, 6-22) in the rapid group; no differences in other secondary outcomes.
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Study conducted in regions with high antimicrobial resistance, which may limit generalizability.
- No difference in primary clinical outcome despite faster antibiotic adjustments.
- Why this matters: These findings suggest that rapid AST may not improve overall clinical outcomes for gram-negative bacteremia, informing clinicians and policymakers about the utility of rapid diagnostics in practice.

deep_dives.title: Red Cell Transfusion in Critically Ill and Noncritically Ill Patients With Acute Myocardial Infarction and Anemia-A Subanalysis of the Myocardial Ischemia and Transfusion (MINT) Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large MINT trial subanalysis, a restrictive transfusion strategy in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia—whether critically ill or not—did not significantly increase 30-day mortality or death/MI compared to a liberal strategy, suggesting restrictive transfusion thresholds are generally safe in this population. This supports the use of restrictive transfusion practices in AMI patients with anemia, including those in the ICU.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: A liberal transfusion strategy in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia is not shown to have different effects between critically ill and noncritically ill patients, with no significant difference in death or death/MI at 30 days.

- Study type: Subgroup analysis of a parallel, two-arm randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adult patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia at 144 sites in the United States, Canada, France, Brazil, New Zealand, and Australia; 1679 (47.9%) were critically ill (ICU at randomization).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Restrictive transfusion strategy (transfuse when hemoglobin < 7-8 g/dL) versus liberal transfusion strategy (transfuse when hemoglobin < 10 g/dL).
- Primary endpoints: Death and the composite of death/myocardial infarction at 30 days.
- Key results: At 30 days, death occurred in 11.9% of critically ill vs. 6.5% of noncritically ill patients; death/MI occurred in 18.9% vs. 12.7%, respectively. Restrictive vs. liberal transfusion resulted in a nonsignificant increase in risk of death (RR 1.09, 95% CI 0.77-1.54 in noncritically ill; RR 1.24, 95% CI 0.95-1.61 in critically ill) and death/MI (RR 1.09, 95% CI 0.85-1.38 in noncritically ill; RR 1.21, 95% CI 0.99-1.47 in critically ill), with no significant interaction between groups.
- Limitations:
- Subgroup analysis may be underpowered for detecting differences between critically ill and noncritically ill patients.
- Results are nonsignificant and confidence intervals are wide.
- ICU status at randomization may not capture all aspects of critical illness.
- Why this matters: This study informs transfusion strategies in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia, suggesting that a liberal approach may be generally favored regardless of critical illness status.

deep_dives.title: Decompression with or without Duraplasty for Chiari I and Syringomyelia.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter, cluster-randomized controlled trial in children with Chiari I malformation and syringomyelia, decompression with duraplasty did not significantly increase surgical complications compared to decompression alone, and while duraplasty led to greater syrinx reduction and fewer repeat surgeries, overall clinical improvement and quality of life were similar; larger studies are needed to clarify the clinical benefits and risks.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: In children with Chiari I malformation and syringomyelia, posterior fossa decompression with duraplasty did not significantly reduce surgical complications compared to decompression alone, though it was associated with greater syrinx reduction and fewer repeat decompressions.

- Study type: Multicenter, cluster-randomized, controlled trial
- Population/setting: Children and young adults (≤21 years) with Chiari I malformation and syringomyelia at 38 centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Posterior fossa decompression with duraplasty (PFD-D) versus posterior fossa decompression alone (PFD)
- Primary endpoints: Surgical complications within 6 months
- Key results: Surgical complications occurred in 14% (PFD-D) vs 6% (PFD) (adjusted OR 2.59; 95% CI, 0.86–7.84; P=0.11); at 24 months, clinical improvement was 58% (PFD-D) vs 46% (PFD), mean syrinx reduction was 3.08±2.33 mm (PFD-D) vs 1.22±1.79 mm (PFD), and repeat decompression was 3% (PFD-D) vs 14% (PFD); health-related quality of life changes were similar.
- Limitations:
- Cluster randomization by center may introduce center-specific biases.
- Sample size may be insufficient to detect small differences in complication rates.
- Long-term outcomes beyond 24 months not reported.
- Why this matters: This study informs surgical decision-making for pediatric Chiari I malformation with syringomyelia, highlighting trade-offs between complication risk and syrinx reduction with or without duraplasty.

deep_dives.title: Airway management with a supraglottic airway device during resuscitation by basic life support providers: a systematic review.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review found low- to very low-certainty evidence that supraglottic airway devices do not improve survival or neurological outcomes compared to bag-valve-mask ventilation when used by basic life support providers during adult cardiac arrest, suggesting no clear clinical benefit for routine SGA use in this context.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: There is no evidence of benefit for supraglottic airway devices compared to bag-valve-mask ventilation by BLS-trained providers during adult cardiac arrest.

- Study type: Systematic review (including randomized trials and observational studies)
- Population/setting: BLS-trained providers managing adult in-hospital or out-of-hospital cardiac arrest; no studies included first rescuers or first responders.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Supraglottic airway devices (SGA) versus bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation
- Primary endpoints: Survival to hospital discharge or 30 days; neurological outcomes; return of spontaneous circulation; chest compression fraction; emesis rates.
- Key results: Randomized trials showed no significant difference in survival to discharge or 30 days (RR 1.28; 95% CI 0.46-3.55) with low certainty; observational studies showed very low-certainty and inconsistent findings for neurological and survival outcomes; one trial found higher chest compression fraction with SGA; emesis rates were similar.
- Limitations:
- Considerable heterogeneity among included studies
- Very low to low certainty of evidence
- No studies involving first rescuers or first responders
- Unable to compare outcomes between different SGA types
- Meta-analysis not feasible for observational studies
- Why this matters: This review informs resuscitation guidelines by highlighting the lack of clear benefit for SGA use over BVM by BLS providers, emphasizing the need for further research and careful consideration before changing airway management practices.

deep_dives.title: Magnesium Sulfate to Prevent Perioperative Atrial Fibrillation in Cardiac Surgery: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This well-conducted randomized controlled trial found that perioperative magnesium sulfate infusion did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation in cardiac surgery patients, indicating that routine prophylactic magnesium supplementation for POAF prevention is not supported.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Perioperative magnesium infusion did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation in cardiac surgery patients and should not be used routinely for this purpose.

- Study type: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, single-center clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adult patients undergoing coronary artery bypass grafting and/or valvular surgery at HagaZiekenhuis, The Hague, The Netherlands, without prior atrial arrhythmias or severe renal dysfunction
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Continuous IV magnesium sulfate infusion (3 mmol/hr, with bolus if baseline magnesium < 1.0 mmol/L) versus placebo (Ringer's lactate), started after anesthesia induction until ICU discharge
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation (POAF)
- Key results: POAF occurred in 37.9% of the magnesium group and 28.6% of the placebo group (relative risk 1.29; 95% CI, 0.92-1.80); no subgroup benefit was observed and no early reduction in POAF was seen; vasopressor use was more frequent in the magnesium group but not statistically significant; no safety signal was identified.
- Limitations:
- Single-center design limits generalizability
- Trial stopped early at interim analysis for futility
- Sample size may be insufficient to detect smaller effects
- Why this matters: This study challenges the routine use of magnesium supplementation to prevent atrial fibrillation after cardiac surgery, informing perioperative management guidelines.

deep_dives.title: Reducing Physical Restraint in the ICU: Multicenter Phase II Randomized Trial of a Novel Device Versus Traditional Wrist Restraints.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter phase II randomized crossover trial of 54 ICU patients, a novel restraint device showed similar patient activity, safety, and satisfaction compared to traditional wrist restraints, suggesting it may be a viable alternative, though larger studies are needed to confirm these findings. This matters clinically as it offers a potential option to reduce reliance on traditional physical restraints without compromising patient safety or satisfaction.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: The novel device did not significantly differ from traditional wrist restraints in upper extremity activity, delirium, sedation, or agitation, and showed moderate satisfaction among patients, families, and clinicians.

- Study type: Within-patient crossover phase II randomized trial
- Population/setting: ICU patients (n = 54) at four U.S. academic medical centers who were verbally responsive, had a provider order for wrist restraints, and expected ICU stay ≥2 days.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Novel restraint device versus traditional wrist restraints, alternated during 4-hour assessment periods over 2 days.
- Primary endpoints: Upper extremity activity measured via wrist actigraphy.
- Key results: Mean activity counts per 4-hour session were similar between the novel device and traditional restraints (relative mean total activity count per session 0.93, 95% CI 0.76-1.13; p = 0.45); 2% potential safety events with the novel device; no significant differences in sedation, agitation, or delirium; 58% of patients/families and 51% of clinicians reported high satisfaction.
- Limitations:
- Small sample size (n = 54).
- Short assessment duration (4-hour sessions over 2 days).
- Limited generalizability beyond verbally responsive ICU patients.
- Satisfaction survey completion rates were not 100%.
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians about the safety and effectiveness of a novel restraint alternative, suggesting it performs similarly to traditional restraints and may guide future restraint reduction strategies in ICUs.

pubmed.title: Effect of immunomodulation on post-cardiac arrest brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials (2605 patients) found that immunomodulatory therapies after cardiac arrest did not significantly improve neurological outcomes or survival, with evidence limited by heterogeneity in interventions and timing; thus, current immunomodulation strategies should not be routinely used for post-cardiac arrest brain injury.

pubmed.title: Postanesthesia Apnea in Former Preterm and Term Infants: A Qualitative Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and meta-analysis found that the risk of postanesthesia apnea in former preterm and term infants decreases with increasing postmenstrual age and later publication year, highlighting the importance of individualized risk assessment and suggesting that monitoring protocols may need updating to reflect these factors. The evidence is strong and clinically relevant for guiding perioperative monitoring and discharge decisions in this population.

pubmed.title: Uniform reporting of outcomes from resuscitation education research: the resuscitation education Utstein style: a consensus report from the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation.
pubmed.bottom_line: This consensus report introduces standardized outcome measures (the resuscitation education Utstein style) for resuscitation education research, developed via a Delphi process, to enable consistent reporting and facilitate stronger evidence synthesis; its adoption is expected to improve the quality and comparability of educational research and ultimately enhance clinical practice.

pubmed.title: Influence of Pre-emptive Haptoglobin on Postoperative Acute Kidney Injury in Cardiac Surgical Patients: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this small, single-center randomized controlled trial, pre-emptive haptoglobin therapy guided by serum-free hemoglobin did not show clear benefit in preventing postoperative acute kidney injury after cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, and the study was stopped early for safety concerns, suggesting limited clinical utility and the need for caution with this approach.

pubmed.title: Remifentanil patient-controlled analgesia for labour analgesia: guidance from the obstetric anaesthetists' association: Recommendations for well tolerated provision of remifentanil PCA for labour analgesia.
pubmed.bottom_line: This expert consensus guidance from the Obstetric Anaesthetists' Association provides evidence-based and graded recommendations for the safe and effective use of remifentanil patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) in labour, addressing previous practice variability and supporting informed, standardized, and patient-centred care where neuraxial analgesia is not suitable; the recommendations are based on a structured literature review and consensus methodology, enhancing their clinical reliability.

pubmed.title: Perioperative management of patients taking sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors: Society for Perioperative Assessment and Quality Improvement (SPAQI) multidisciplinary consensus statement.
pubmed.bottom_line: This multidisciplinary consensus statement provides updated, evidence-informed recommendations for perioperative management of SGLT2 inhibitors, advocating a tailored approach based on diabetes status, comorbidities, and surgical factors to reduce the risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis; these guidelines offer stronger clinical direction than previous variable recommendations and are important for improving patient safety.

pubmed.title: Effect of vascular access strategy on long-term outcomes in patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: a randomised clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized clinical trial of adults with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, initial intraosseous versus intravenous vascular access resulted in similar 1-year survival and neurological outcomes, indicating no clinically significant difference between the two strategies for long-term patient outcomes.

pubmed.title: Economic evidence for licensed ready-to-administer intravenous products compared with standard vials and ampoules: a systematic review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review found that ready-to-administer intravenous products generally reduce preparation errors, drug wastage, and staff time compared to standard vials, with potential economic savings; however, the evidence is mostly from small, single-center observational studies with variable quality, so clinical and financial benefits may be context-dependent.

pubmed.title: Phenotypic clustering of myocardial infarction complicated by out-of-hospital cardiac arrest using unsupervised machine learning.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective cohort study used unsupervised machine learning to identify three distinct clinical phenotypes among patients with myocardial infarction complicated by out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, each with significantly different 90-day mortality risks; this data-driven stratification may improve risk assessment and guide individualized management in this high-risk population.

pubmed.title: Pain management after elective caesarean section under neuraxial anaesthesia: an updated systematic review and procedure-specific postoperative pain management (PROSPECT) recommendations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This updated systematic review of 61 randomized controlled trials provides strong evidence-based recommendations for pain management after elective caesarean section under neuraxial anesthesia, emphasizing intrathecal morphine or diamorphine, regular paracetamol and NSAIDs, dexamethasone, and specific surgical techniques, which together optimize postoperative analgesia and minimize opioid use—important for improving maternal recovery and safety.

pubmed.title: Geographic inequalities in prehospital critical care for cardiac arrest: a spatial analysis of deprivation and service configuration.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large spatial analysis found that geographic distance to prehospital critical care bases, rather than neighborhood deprivation, primarily determines critical care team attendance for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in England, highlighting the need for service configuration changes to address access inequalities.

pubmed.title: Peri-operative fasting in adults: an international, multidisciplinary consensus statement.
pubmed.bottom_line: This international consensus statement, based on systematic review and Delphi methodology, recommends maintaining current fasting guidelines for solids but supports encouraging clear liquids up to 2 hours before anesthesia and advocates for even more liberal clear liquid intake where institutional protocols permit, aiming to reduce patient harm from prolonged fasting and improve perioperative outcomes. These updated, multidisciplinary recommendations provide strong, globally relevant guidance to optimize perioperative fasting practices in adults.

pubmed.title: Association of perioperative fluid balance and acute kidney injury in patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery: A pre-planned secondary analysis of a multicentre prospective observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter prospective observational study of 1,139 patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery, higher perioperative fluid balance was strongly associated with an increased risk of postoperative acute kidney injury, highlighting the clinical importance of careful fluid management to reduce AKI risk in this population.

pubmed.title: Healthcare costs of expedited transport compared with standard care following out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: results from the EVIDENCE randomised controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This randomized controlled trial found no significant difference in healthcare costs between expedited transport and standard care after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, but care during ECPR-available (business) hours was more expensive; these findings suggest that expedited transport does not increase costs, but timing of care impacts resource use.

pubmed.title: Pro: we should fully utilise the preload reserve in high-risk patients undergoing noncardiac surgery.
pubmed.bottom_line: Titrated fluid challenges to optimize preload in high-risk patients undergoing noncardiac surgery do not increase cumulative fluid balance and may prevent hemodynamic instability and hypoperfusion; recent guidelines support this approach as a physiologically sound and clinically appropriate standard of care.

pubmed.title: Con: we should fully utilise the preload reserve in high-risk patients undergoing noncardiac surgery.
pubmed.bottom_line: This expert opinion argues that routinely maximizing preload reserve with fluid boluses in high-risk noncardiac surgical patients is not supported by physiological principles or clinical need, emphasizing that fluid administration should be individualized and not based solely on fluid responsiveness; this matters clinically to avoid unnecessary fluid overload and its associated risks.

pubmed.title: The multifaceted neural architecture of pain modulation by emotion and cognition: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 100 neuroimaging studies (n=2735) reveals that emotion and cognition modulate pain through distinct neural pathways, with similar subjective pain changes arising from different brain activation patterns; this underscores the complexity of pain modulation and highlights the need for individualized approaches in pain management.

pubmed.title: Key targets for activating anaesthesia shared decision-making: a mixed methods systematic review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This mixed methods systematic review identifies nine key clinician, patient, and system-level targets to activate and embed shared decision-making in anaesthesia, highlighting the need for structured communication training, explicit patient engagement, and workflow integration; while evidence is limited to eleven studies, these findings provide actionable priorities to improve consistency and quality of peri-operative patient involvement.

pubmed.title: Peri-operative cardiac arrest guidelines: time to include liver transplantation.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article highlights the need to update peri-operative cardiac arrest guidelines to specifically address the unique challenges of liver transplantation, emphasizing a gap in current protocols that could impact patient outcomes in this high-risk population.

pubmed.title: Long-term neurological outcomes after extracorporeal cardiopulmonary resuscitation for refractory cardiac arrest: a 14-year single-centre cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large single-center retrospective cohort study, only 17.3% of adults receiving ECPR for refractory cardiac arrest had good neurological outcomes at six months, with younger age, initial shockable rhythm, and shorter low-flow time as key predictors; these findings underscore the need for careful patient selection to optimize long-term neurological recovery.

pubmed.title: Time of day of cardiac surgery and postoperative outcomes in the UK: a secondary analysis of linked national datasets.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large UK secondary analysis found a modestly increased hazard of cardiovascular death for cardiac surgeries performed in the late morning compared to other times, but no differences in other major outcomes, suggesting that time of day may influence mortality risk and should be considered in perioperative planning, though the effect size is small.

pubmed.title: Electrical impedance tomography-derived flow index during spontaneous breathing trial stratifies the risk of reintubation within 48 h after extubation.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective multicenter study of 150 mechanically ventilated adults, an electrical impedance tomography-derived flow index (EFI) measured during spontaneous breathing trials accurately predicted both SBT failure and the risk of reintubation within 48 hours after extubation, suggesting EFI may be a valuable, noninvasive tool for identifying patients at higher risk of early extubation failure.

pubmed.title: Effect of ethnicity and parity on utilisation of labour epidural analgesia: a retrospective study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study from New Zealand found that Māori and especially Pacific women were significantly less likely than European women to receive epidural analgesia during labour, particularly in subsequent births, indicating persistent ethnic disparities in access to effective labour pain management and underscoring the need to address potential barriers to equitable care.

pubmed.title: The Minnesota Mobile Resuscitation Consortium ECMO truck: a feasibility study for ECPR in refractory out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
pubmed.bottom_line: This small feasibility study found that delivering prehospital ECPR via a mobile ECMO truck for refractory out-of-hospital cardiac arrest is feasible and achieves neurologically favorable survival rates comparable to in-hospital ECPR when matched for low-flow time, suggesting mobile ECPR could expand access to advanced resuscitation in geographically distant patients, though larger studies are needed to confirm these findings.

pubmed.title: Association Between Emergency Department Undertriage or Overtriage With Timeliness of Care and Patient Outcomes.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that both undertriage and overtriage in the emergency department are associated with small but measurable delays in care, highlighting the importance of accurate triage and suggesting that incorporating patient history data could improve early identification of critically ill patients. These findings underscore the clinical need to optimize triage processes to minimize delays and improve patient outcomes.

foamed.title: Above the Ears
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article discusses the importance of securing endotracheal tubes above the ears and around the occiput, rather than circumferentially around the neck, in patients with severe traumatic brain injury to avoid impeding cerebral venous outflow and potentially worsening intracranial pressure; no new clinical data are presented.

foamed.title: EMCrit 426 – Dave Carr – Tips & Tricks from his Career in Emergency Medicine
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This EMCrit episode features educational commentary and practical tips from Dr. David Carr’s career in emergency medicine, without presenting new clinical study data; it includes reference to an external randomized clinical trial on active versus passive voiding trials but does not itself report original research findings.

foamed.title: CT Case 108
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In this case, a 50-year-old male with prior hernia repair presented with left lower quadrant pain and was found on CT to have a 5.7 cm jejuno-jejunal intussusception without a lead point, bowel obstruction, or signs of ischemia, highlighting a rare but important diagnosis in adults.

deep_dives.title: Effect of immunomodulation on post-cardiac arrest brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials (2605 patients) found that immunomodulatory therapies after cardiac arrest did not significantly improve neurological outcomes or survival, with evidence limited by heterogeneity in interventions and timing; thus, current immunomodulation strategies should not be routinely used for post-cardiac arrest brain injury.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** This systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials (2605 patients) found that immunomodulatory therapies after cardiac arrest did not significantly improve neurological outcomes or survival, with evidence limited by heterogeneity in interventions and timing; thus, current immunomodulation strategies should not be routinely used for post-cardiac arrest brain injury.

BOTTOM LINE: Immunomodulatory therapies after cardiac arrest did not significantly improve neurological outcomes or survival.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
- Population/setting: Adults resuscitated after in- and out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (N=2605) from 11 studies and 3 sub-studies
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Immunomodulatory interventions (mainly corticosteroids, interleukin-6 receptor blockade, calcineurin inhibition) versus standard care or placebo
- Primary endpoints: Favorable neurological outcome, 30-day mortality, and inflammatory biomarker levels
- Key results: No significant association with improved neurological outcome (RR 1.04 [0.85, 1.28]) or reduced mortality (RR 0.97 [0.91, 1.03]); trend toward decreased IL-6 levels at 24 hours after glucocorticoid administration.
- Limitations:
- Heterogeneity in interventions, dosages, and timing
- Variation in outcome and biomarker assessment
- Limited direct comparability between studies
- Why this matters: Current immunomodulatory therapies do not provide clear benefit for neurological or survival outcomes after cardiac arrest, highlighting the need for more targeted research.

deep_dives.title: Effect of vascular access strategy on long-term outcomes in patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: a randomised clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized clinical trial of adults with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, initial intraosseous versus intravenous vascular access resulted in similar 1-year survival and neurological outcomes, indicating no clinically significant difference between the two strategies for long-term patient outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Initial intraosseous and intravenous vascular access strategies yield similar long-term survival and neurological outcomes in adults with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.

- Study type: Randomised clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest requiring vascular access
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Initial intraosseous vascular access versus initial intravenous vascular access
- Primary endpoints: Survival, survival with favourable neurological outcome (modified Rankin Scale 0-3), and health-related quality of life (EQ-5D-5L) at 6 months and 1 year
- Key results: At 1 year, survival was 11% for intraosseous and 9% for intravenous groups (risk ratio 1.24; 95% CI 0.91-1.67); favourable neurological outcome was 10% vs 8% (risk ratio 1.28; 95% CI 0.93-1.77); mean EQ-5D-5L score among survivors was 83 vs 76 (mean difference 7; 95% CI 1-13).
- Limitations:
- Three patients lost to follow-up for 1-year survival
- Confidence intervals include the possibility of no difference
- Results may not generalize to traumatic cardiac arrest or pediatric populations
- Why this matters: This study informs emergency care providers that either intraosseous or intravenous access can be used for vascular access during adult out-of-hospital cardiac arrest without significant differences in long-term outcomes.

deep_dives.title: Association of perioperative fluid balance and acute kidney injury in patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery: A pre-planned secondary analysis of a multicentre prospective observational study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large multicenter prospective observational study of 1,139 patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery, higher perioperative fluid balance was strongly associated with an increased risk of postoperative acute kidney injury, highlighting the clinical importance of careful fluid management to reduce AKI risk in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Higher positive perioperative fluid balance during the first 24 hours is associated with an increased risk of postoperative acute kidney injury in patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery.

- Study type: Prospective observational study; pre-planned secondary analysis of a multicentre study.
- Population/setting: Patients undergoing elective primary colorectal surgery with planned overnight stay in 80 hospitals in Spain between September and December 2017 (N=1139).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Higher perioperative fluid balance (quartiles Q3 and Q4) compared to the lowest quartile (Q1).
- Primary endpoints: Occurrence of acute kidney injury (AKI) at 30 days following surgery, defined by KDIGO and EPCO guidelines.
- Key results: 6.4% (73/1139) developed AKI; adjusted relative risks for AKI were 4.10 (95% CI, 1.60–10.51) for Q3 and 4.81 (95% CI, 1.91–12.11) for Q4 compared to Q1; higher positive fluid balance was significantly associated with increased AKI risk (P < 0.01).
- Limitations:
- Secondary analysis of an observational study.
- Potential for residual confounding.
- Exclusion of patients with incomplete data or severe renal impairment.
- Why this matters: Understanding the relationship between perioperative fluid management and AKI risk can inform strategies to optimize fluid therapy and reduce postoperative complications in colorectal surgery patients.

deep_dives.title: Influence of Pre-emptive Haptoglobin on Postoperative Acute Kidney Injury in Cardiac Surgical Patients: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this small, single-center randomized controlled trial, pre-emptive haptoglobin therapy guided by serum-free hemoglobin did not show clear benefit in preventing postoperative acute kidney injury after cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, and the study was stopped early for safety concerns, suggesting limited clinical utility and the need for caution with this approach.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pre-emptive haptoglobin administration in cardiac surgery patients increased postoperative creatinine and was associated with worse renal outcomes.

- Study type: Single-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adult patients undergoing major cardiovascular surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass at a single center
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Pre-emptive haptoglobin therapy (4000 U when serum-free hemoglobin reached 0.05 g/dL) versus standard of care (4000 U haptoglobin only if hemolytic urine was confirmed)
- Primary endpoints: Difference between preoperative creatinine and maximum creatinine within 48 hours after surgery (ΔCr)
- Key results: Median ΔCr was 0.20 (0.05-0.44) in the pre-emptive group and 0.14 (0.04-0.19) in the standard care group (P = .05); pre-emptive haptoglobin significantly increased ΔCr in multivariable analysis (P = .03).
- Limitations:
- Study was terminated early due to safety concerns.
- Single-center design limits generalizability.
- Small sample size (34 and 33 patients per group).
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Why this matters: This study suggests that pre-emptive haptoglobin administration may worsen renal outcomes in cardiac surgery patients, challenging its use for preventing acute kidney injury in this setting.

deep_dives.title: The multifaceted neural architecture of pain modulation by emotion and cognition: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 100 neuroimaging studies (n=2735) reveals that emotion and cognition modulate pain through distinct neural pathways, with similar subjective pain changes arising from different brain activation patterns; this underscores the complexity of pain modulation and highlights the need for individualized approaches in pain management.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pain modulation by emotion and cognition involves multiple neural mechanisms, with different brain activation patterns supporting diverse strategies for pain control.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of functional neuroimaging studies
- Population/setting: Healthy individuals; 100 studies with 2735 participants
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Contextual modulation of pain (e.g., attentional, expectation, introspection/self-referential tasks) compared to baseline or control conditions
- Primary endpoints: Brain activity changes in response to pain modulation as measured by functional neuroimaging
- Key results: Three main neural patterns were identified: (1) prefrontal activation with sensory area modulation (deactivation in hypoalgesia, hyperactivation in hyperalgesia), (2) prefrontal activation without nociceptive area changes (perceptual decision bias), and (3) ventromedial prefrontal involvement with deactivation in dorsolateral/ventrolateral prefrontal areas during introspective/self-referential tasks.
- Limitations:
- Exclusion of studies on chronic pain
- Potential heterogeneity in neuroimaging methods and task paradigms
- Risk of bias assessed but not detailed in results
- Why this matters: Understanding the diverse neural mechanisms underlying pain modulation can inform the development of targeted behavioral and neuromodulation-based pain therapies.

deep_dives.title: Healthcare costs of expedited transport compared with standard care following out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: results from the EVIDENCE randomised controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This randomized controlled trial found no significant difference in healthcare costs between expedited transport and standard care after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, but care during ECPR-available (business) hours was more expensive; these findings suggest that expedited transport does not increase costs, but timing of care impacts resource use.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Expedited transport and standard care for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest resulted in similar health system costs, but care during ECPR-available hours was more expensive.

- Study type: Prospective costing analysis alongside a randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 197 randomized participants with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in Greater Sydney, Australia
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Expedited transport versus standard care; comparison also by timing (inside vs. outside ECPR-available hours)
- Primary endpoints: Mean total and per-participant healthcare use and costs from paramedic contact to hospital discharge or death
- Key results: Mean costs per patient were AUD$60,780 for expedited transport and $67,579 for standard care (difference: -$6,799; 95% CI: -$44,014 to $30,416; not significant). Mean costs were $36,182 higher within ECPR hours compared to outside (95% CI: $1,554–$72,918).
- Limitations:
- Single metropolitan region may limit generalizability
- Cost differences not statistically significant
- Potential variability in ECPR availability and implementation
- Why this matters: Understanding the cost implications of different pre-hospital transport strategies can inform resource allocation and policy decisions for cardiac arrest care.

pubmed.title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.

pubmed.title: Enteral When Compared With IV Magnesium Replacement in the Critically Ill: A Noninferiority Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.

pubmed.title: An Exploration of "Near-Miss" Events in Non-Operating Room Anesthesia Locations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective cohort study found a 3.2% incidence of near-miss events in non-operating room anesthesia (NORA) settings, with environmental factors—especially poor group dynamics—being the most common contributors; these findings highlight the need for targeted safety interventions and improved team communication in NORA environments.

pubmed.title: Regional Alveolar Damage Despite Lung Protective Ventilation Settings During Robotic-Assisted Laparoscopic Surgery.
pubmed.bottom_line: This small observational study found evidence of regional alveolar injury in dependent lung areas during robotic-assisted laparoscopic surgery, even when standard lung protective ventilation settings were used, suggesting that current ventilation strategies may not fully prevent perioperative lung damage in this context and may warrant reconsideration of intraoperative ventilatory management.

pubmed.title: Plant-Based Diet and Risk of Sepsis: A 16-Year Follow-Up Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective UK Biobank cohort study found that greater adherence to a healthy plant-based diet was associated with a significantly lower risk of sepsis, while unhealthy plant-based diets increased risk, suggesting that diet quality—not just plant-based content—matters for sepsis prevention; evidence is strong due to the large sample size and robust adjustments, making these findings clinically relevant for dietary counseling.

pubmed.title: Does the Frailty Burden Impact ICU Performance Indicators Benchmarking? A Multicenter Cohort Study Using Two Severity-of-Illness Scores.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter cohort study, ICU frailty burden did not significantly impact standardized mortality or resource use indicators when using or recalibrating common severity-of-illness scores, suggesting that current benchmarking metrics remain valid regardless of frailty prevalence. This supports continued use of these performance indicators without adjustment for frailty in ICU benchmarking.

pubmed.title: Outcomes of cardiac arrest occurred in detention: a French nationwide study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective French study found that cardiac arrest survival at 30 days is significantly lower in incarcerated individuals compared to the general population, highlighting a need for targeted prevention and improved emergency response in detention settings.

pubmed.title: Postoperative hypotension in patients recovering from noncardiac surgery: a prospective, blinded observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective observational study found that postoperative hypotension (MAP <65 mm Hg) is common but typically not severe or prolonged after noncardiac surgery; however, its presence is associated with increased rates of acute kidney injury and unplanned ICU admissions, highlighting the need for vigilant postoperative blood pressure monitoring.

pubmed.title: ETHER: a proposal for a European peri-operative data sharing and registry network.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article proposes the creation of a European peri-operative data sharing and registry network to standardize and integrate clinical data across Europe, aiming to improve outcomes research, quality benchmarking, and policy evaluation; while not presenting new clinical evidence, it highlights a significant infrastructure initiative that could enhance peri-operative care and research capabilities.

pubmed.title: Does peripartum intravenous calcium administration reduce the occurrence of uterine atony in caesarean sections?: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that peripartum intravenous calcium administration reduces the occurrence of uterine atony during caesarean sections, suggesting a potentially effective intervention to decrease postpartum hemorrhage risk; however, the strength of evidence and clinical recommendations depend on the quality and size of included studies.

pubmed.title: HEAVEN criteria to predict difficult in-hospital rapid sequence intubation: a prospective single-centre observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective single-centre observational study suggests that the HEAVEN criteria may be useful for predicting difficult in-hospital rapid sequence intubation, providing clinicians with a potentially valuable tool for airway management, though further validation in broader settings is needed.

pubmed.title: Telemedicine Use and Outcomes Following Discharge From the Emergency Department, 2020-2022.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study, telemedicine follow-up after emergency department discharge for chronic conditions was used infrequently but was not associated with higher rates of return hospitalization compared to in-person follow-up, suggesting telemedicine is a safe alternative for post-ED care in appropriately selected patients.

pubmed.title: Cardiopulmonary fitness in patients undergoing major hepato-pancreatobiliary surgery who are non-anaemic and iron replete vs. non-anaemic and iron deficient: an observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This observational study suggests that non-anaemic but iron-deficient patients undergoing major hepato-pancreatobiliary surgery may have reduced cardiopulmonary fitness compared to iron-replete counterparts, highlighting the potential clinical importance of assessing and addressing iron deficiency even in the absence of anaemia.

pubmed.title: Carbon emissions from total intravenous vs. volatile anaesthesia for elective cholecystectomy: a pilot observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This pilot observational study suggests that total intravenous anaesthesia produces lower carbon emissions compared to volatile anaesthesia for elective cholecystectomy, highlighting a potential environmental benefit that may inform anaesthetic choice, though larger studies are needed to confirm these findings.

pubmed.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab vs Observation in Resected Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, randomized phase 3 trial found that adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free or overall survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer, leading to early trial termination for futility; these results suggest adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used in this setting.

pubmed.title: Perioperative serplulimab with neoadjuvant chemotherapy versus perioperative chemotherapy in PD-L1-positive gastric cancer (ASTRUM-006): a randomised, double-blind, multicentre, phase 3 study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, adding perioperative serplulimab (a PD-1 inhibitor) to neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy significantly improved event-free survival in patients with PD-L1-positive, locally advanced, resectable gastric or gastro-oesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, suggesting a new standard of care for this population.

foamed.title: Personalized Hemodynamic Resuscitation Targeting Capillary Refill Time in Early Septic Shock: The ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 Randomized Clinical Trial
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 trial, a personalized hemodynamic resuscitation protocol targeting capillary refill time in early septic shock was superior to usual care for a hierarchical composite outcome (Win ratio 1.16, 95% CI 1.04–1.28), but did not reduce 28-day mortality (26.5% vs. 26.6%).

foamed.title: Should I treat Strep throat?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational discussion exploring the evidence and uncertainties surrounding the treatment of streptococcal pharyngitis, including the benefits and limitations of antibiotics, but does not present new clinical data or study results.

foamed.title: OpenAnesthesia – International Anesthesia Research Society
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This content is an informational announcement about membership benefits, including access to OpenAnesthesia, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.

BOTTOM LINE: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials probably reduce the incidence of early and overall VAP in mechanically ventilated patients, but do not reduce in-hospital mortality.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
- Population/setting: Mechanically ventilated patients in ICUs; 11 RCTs with 5562 patients
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials versus standard care or placebo
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), in-hospital mortality, duration of mechanical ventilation, length of hospital stay, drug resistance, and adverse events
- Key results: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials reduced VAP incidence (RR 0.65, 95% CI 0.55-0.77) and early VAP (RR 0.52, 95% CI 0.41-0.66), but did not reduce in-hospital mortality (RR 0.85, 95% CI 0.68-1.06), duration of mechanical ventilation (MD -0.32 days, 95% CI -0.79 to 0.16), or length of hospital stay (MD -1.56 days, 95% CI -5.15 to 2.02); no increased risk of drug resistance or adverse events was observed.
- Limitations:
- Incomplete safety reporting
- Low certainty for some outcomes (e.g., length of hospital stay)
- Need for comprehensive resistance surveillance and cost-effectiveness analyses
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians that while systemic prophylactic antibacterials can reduce VAP incidence in mechanically ventilated patients, they do not improve mortality, highlighting the need for careful consideration of benefits versus risks and further research on safety and cost-effectiveness.

deep_dives.title: Enteral When Compared With IV Magnesium Replacement in the Critically Ill: A Noninferiority Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.

BOTTOM LINE: Enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for correcting hypomagnesemia but offered advantages in cost, environmental impact, and reduced urinary magnesium loss.

- Study type: Prospective, open-label, randomized, noninferiority trial
- Population/setting: Critically ill patients with serum magnesium 0.35–0.7 mmol/L in a single-center mixed medical-surgical-trauma ICU
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Enteral magnesium replacement versus IV magnesium replacement
- Primary endpoints: Serum magnesium concentration at 24 hours after enrollment (noninferiority margin 0.1 mmol/L)
- Key results: At 24 hours, mean magnesium was 0.80 mmol/L (enteral) vs 0.92 mmol/L (IV), mean difference -0.12 mmol/L (95% CI, -0.16 to -0.07), not confirming noninferiority; enteral replacement reduced urinary magnesium, cost, waste, carbon emissions, and IV fluid use, with no differences in other clinical outcomes.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Open-label design
- Noninferiority margin may affect interpretation
- Results may not generalize to severe hypomagnesemia or other settings
- Why this matters: Enteral magnesium replacement may be a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable alternative to IV therapy for mild-to-moderate hypomagnesemia, though it may be less effective in rapidly correcting serum magnesium.

deep_dives.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab vs Observation in Resected Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large, randomized phase 3 trial found that adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free or overall survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer, leading to early trial termination for futility; these results suggest adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer after standard adjuvant therapy.

- Study type: Open-label, randomized phase 3 clinical trial
- Population/setting: Patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer (tumors ≥4 cm and/or lymph node positive, without EGFR/ALK sensitizing mutations), enrolled at 378 centers in the US National Clinical Trials Network from May 2016 to September 2019.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks for up to 1 year versus standard care observation.
- Primary endpoints: Co-primary endpoints were disease-free survival in the intention-to-treat population and in those with tumoral PD-L1 expression ≥50%.
- Key results: Median disease-free survival was 71.3 months with nivolumab and 68.8 months with observation (HR 0.97, 97% CI 0.79-1.20; 1-sided P = .39); in the PD-L1 ≥50% subgroup, median DFS was 89.8 vs 78.5 months (HR 0.86, 98% CI 0.55-1.34; 1-sided P = .22). The trial was stopped for futility at 75% information.
- Limitations:
- Trial stopped early for futility.
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Results may not generalize to patients with EGFR or ALK mutations.
- Why this matters: These findings suggest that adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used after surgery and standard adjuvant therapy in resected non-small cell lung cancer without EGFR/ALK alterations.
